Page 27, Remark 4.1 (end of Section 4, before Appendix A).

Remark 4.1. The question of convergence of S-saddle points of the particle system to those of the limit process is more elaborate and will be addressed in a forthcoming paper.
title: Convergence of saddle-point stopping times for interacting mean-field Dynkin games
background_latex:
Consider a filtered probability space supporting the noises needed to define the payoff evaluation and the associated backward stochastic differential equations (BSDEs). Fix a finite horizon \([0,T]\).

A (zero-sum) Dynkin game is a two-player stopping game in which Player 1 chooses a stopping time \(\tau\) and Player 2 chooses a stopping time \(\sigma\), and a payoff is realized at \(\tau\wedge\sigma\). In the mean-field extension considered here, the running and terminal payoffs may depend on the current game value process and on its law.

Mean-field game value process: Let \(V=(V_t)_{0\le t\le T}\) denote the (common) value process of a mean-field Dynkin game, when it exists.

Interacting particle approximation: For each integer \(n\ge 1\), consider an \(n\)-player (weakly interacting) system producing value processes \(V^{i,n}=(V^{i,n}_t)_{0\le t\le T}\) for \(i=1,\dots,n\). The interaction is through the empirical measure \(L_n[V^n_t]:=\frac1n\sum_{j=1}^n \delta_{V^{j,n}_t}\), where \(V^n_t=(V^{1,n}_t,\dots,V^{n,n}_t)\).

Stopping rules defined by hitting the obstacles: Let \(h_1(t,y,\mu)\) and \(h_2(t,y,\mu)\) be given lower/upper obstacle functions (barriers), with \(h_1\le h_2\). When a value process \(V\) is given, define candidate saddle-point stopping times by the first hitting times of the barriers:
\[
\tau^\star_S := \inf\{t\ge S: V_t = h_1(t,V_t,\mathcal L(V_t))\},\qquad
\sigma^\star_S := \inf\{t\ge S: V_t = h_2(t,V_t,\mathcal L(V_t))\},
\]
where \(S\) is a stopping time and \(\mathcal L(V_t)\) denotes the law of \(V_t\).

Similarly, for the interacting \(n\)-particle system, define for each \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) and each stopping time \(S\):
\[
\tau^{\star,i,n}_S := \inf\{t\ge S: V^{i,n}_t = h_1(t,V^{i,n}_t, L_n[V^n_t])\},\qquad
\sigma^{\star,i,n}_S := \inf\{t\ge S: V^{i,n}_t = h_2(t,V^{i,n}_t, L_n[V^n_t])\}.
\]

Propagation of chaos for values: As \(n\to\infty\), one studies convergence of the finite-dimensional laws of \((V^{1,n},\dots,V^{k,n})\) to \(k\) i.i.d. copies of the mean-field value process \(V\) (propagation of chaos). The question below asks for an analogous convergence statement for the associated saddle-point stopping times.
problem_definition_latex:
Assume the mean-field Dynkin game admits a saddle point given by the barrier-hitting stopping times \((\tau^\star_S,\sigma^\star_S)\) defined from its value process \(V\), and that for each \(n\) the interacting Dynkin game admits saddle points \((\tau^{\star,i,n}_S,\sigma^{\star,i,n}_S)\) defined from \(V^{i,n}\) and the empirical measures \(L_n[V^n]\).

Determine whether, and under what conditions, the saddle-point stopping times of the particle system converge to those of the mean-field limit as \(n\to\infty\), in an appropriate sense (e.g. convergence in law or in probability of \((\tau^{\star,1,n}_S,\sigma^{\star,1,n}_S,\dots,\tau^{\star,k,n}_S,\sigma^{\star,k,n}_S)\) to \((\tau^{\star,1}_S,\sigma^{\star,1}_S,\dots,\tau^{\star,k}_S,\sigma^{\star,k}_S)\) for each fixed \(k\), where \((\tau^{\star,i}_S,\sigma^{\star,i}_S)\) are i.i.d. copies of \((\tau^\star_S,\sigma^\star_S)\)).
latex:
Consider a filtered probability space supporting the noises needed to define the payoff evaluation and the associated backward stochastic differential equations (BSDEs). Fix a finite horizon \([0,T]\).

A (zero-sum) Dynkin game is a two-player stopping game in which Player 1 chooses a stopping time \(\tau\) and Player 2 chooses a stopping time \(\sigma\), and a payoff is realized at \(\tau\wedge\sigma\). In the mean-field extension considered here, the running and terminal payoffs may depend on the current game value process and on its law.

Mean-field game value process: Let \(V=(V_t)_{0\le t\le T}\) denote the (common) value process of a mean-field Dynkin game, when it exists.

Interacting particle approximation: For each integer \(n\ge 1\), consider an \(n\)-player (weakly interacting) system producing value processes \(V^{i,n}=(V^{i,n}_t)_{0\le t\le T}\) for \(i=1,\dots,n\). The interaction is through the empirical measure \(L_n[V^n_t]:=\frac1n\sum_{j=1}^n \delta_{V^{j,n}_t}\), where \(V^n_t=(V^{1,n}_t,\dots,V^{n,n}_t)\).

\[

\[
\tau^{\star,i,n}_S := \inf\{t\ge S: V^{i,n}_t = h_1(t,V^{i,n}_t, L_n[V^n_t])\},\qquad
\sigma^{\star,i,n}_S := \inf\{t\ge S: V^{i,n}_t = h_2(t,V^{i,n}_t, L_n[V^n_t])\}.

Assume the mean-field Dynkin game admits a saddle point given by the barrier-hitting stopping times \((\tau^\star_S,\sigma^\star_S)\) defined from its value process \(V\), and that for each \(n\) the interacting Dynkin game admits saddle points \((\tau^{\star,i,n}_S,\sigma^{\star,i,n}_S)\) defined from \(V^{i,n}\) and the empirical measures \(L_n[V^n]\).

open_problem_quote: Remark 4.1. The question of convergence of S-saddle points of the particle system to those of the limit process is more elaborate and will be addressed in a forthcoming paper.
subject_classification: Mean-field stochastic control and stopping games (Dynkin games), BSDE methods
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is qualitative and does not specify a concrete class of coefficients or a precise convergence mode to falsify; constructing counterexamples would require delicate stochastic analysis and is not a finite combinatorial search.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: There is no clear automatically checkable fitness signal for candidate proofs/conditions, and verifying convergence of stopping times in such stochastic game/BSDE settings is not readily automatable without substantial theorem-proving infrastructure.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
